speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 7, part 2, interview with Wanda Mehr. You were telling us about the beginning of prison, the-- all these women in this very small room. Did they issue you any clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What one wears. As a matter of fact, I would say there was, and it, hard it was, I was quite happy when they, they didn't put us in like that. They threw us in, that we-- there was no room to fell on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we fell one over the other. They threw me in and the people there, they were mostly Polish prisoners, some Russian, as I mentioned before, after the, the Russian Army left, officer's wives as political prisoners. When they, when they threw me out, one said, loud, another Russian is here. I was quite happy that I'm Russian, not Jewish. Another Russian is here. I don't know why they said, if I looked like Russian. Maybe my broad face? So I was quite happy that I'm Russian, and not Jewish. Did they give you a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they cut your hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing. No. No. It was too much for them to bother with us as we were. But there was no drinking water. You couldn't go out to a toilet. Everything had to done in that tiny little room. With so many women, had to be that no cleaning, no washing. And you know with women menstruating, everything just under one's self. There was only-- they put in one-- once a day, in the morning, they put in a, a barrel, a big barrel. That was a toilet. And whoever need it, maybe just in this time, there was no need for it, everything is at barrel, before everybody. And then they took it out and that was it. Otherwise it was all on the floor, mingled, mixed everything, blood and-- and everything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they give you any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food at all, but once in the morning, a tiny piece of very hard bread, a tiny piece. You couldn't bite it even. You could-- I didn't-- never ate it. It was impossible. Old, stale, tiny piece of bread, and that was for breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for lunch they gave us-- I never ate it either, because it was a soup, a kind of borscht. It should be from red-- how do you call [NON-ENGLISH]? Beets. Beets. Beetroots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, beetroot. Yeah, it should have been, but it was from the peelings only. Not the beetroot. The beetroot itself, they kept for themselves. Only the peelings, with soil on it from the soil, not washed. And that soup smelled terribly, and dirty. So I don't know if anyone ate it. I know that I couldn't touch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't eat, and I didn't-- no fresh drinking water. They didn't take any water. How I survived, I don't know. A half a year I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every morning, I have seen it, and if someone tells me it's not true, I have seen it myself. They took in those Russian women who were-- as political prisoners, wives of the Russian officers who left, left and left them behind. They took in-- each morning, they took another one in, and one came out. I don't think that she could cry. She didn't have any energy to, to cry, a woman, young woman, maybe 30, for about 20 minutes or longer. It was a door leading to the Chief Gestapo and SS men there. They took her in, and she came back about, from there, about 20, 25 minutes later. And her whole skin and muscles were hanging in strips. I've seen it, bloody, in strips. They used to beat them up there. I don't know what they used, some sharp object. They were-- I have seen her skin and muscles hanging like strips on the bones. She still lived, but she didn't cry. She couldn't cry, maybe. She was half alive. That's how they beat her there. And I, I can, could see myself when they will take me in-- they took in, every day, another one-- that they would do with me the same. And as I, as I said, I was lucky. They didn't. But I was there about-- first, they came night time as I was standing and hit me with something sharp on my head-- again, I have quite a lot of, visible still, scars-- hit me with something sharp on my head in my sleep, standing, sleeping. Ask me in German [GERMAN], because they knew that some-- they-- if I had an assumed name, I won't remember if they hit me middle of the night in sleep. They thought so, then I'll tell them my name, my real name, Jewish name. But I didn't. I never, never-- even if I would be hit 100 times, I never-- I forgot that I was once [INAUDIBLE]. I said, Wanda Grabowienska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they ask me again and again, what is your name? No. That's not your name. They thought that I'm losing, from hitting, my memory, on the head. And again, what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two of them. And I said Wanda Grabowienska, and I couldn't even speak. I said, Wanda Grabowienska. Then they let me-- they left me and went out. Then I was-- next day, I was bleeding all over, and I did nothing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day, no doctor attended me, nothing. I didn't have a, a scrap of linen to put on top, to, to, you know, to cover it. Next day, I have, had very bad scars there, and a doctor, he has seen it, a German doctor. What was his name? I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go to a German doctor, they-- because I told him about-- and I had no, no witnesses. But I described the prison so well and so exactly that they believe me. They don't believe easily. You have to have witnesses. I didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told him, I don't know. Do you know if anyone survived [NON-ENGLISH]? I have seen-- that we all stood in [INAUDIBLE]. As we stood around near each other, huddled together, a tiny little window from the second floor, it was on second floor, down to the back yard, we have seen them killing people. They killed Poles, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they had a long nose, or they had dark hair, they didn't believe. One screamed-- she was in our, in our room-- and said, I give you the address. My parents live there. Go-- they got her on the street. Go over there Lwow they live, my parents there. And they didn't bother. I'm not, I'm not Jewish. I'm a Catholic, Polish. They didn't believe her. They killed her. We have seen it. They killed her. Once they had something on-- on, they thought on me, it's enough to be killed. And I was lucky. Then one day, they took me in there, and I thought, all right if they kill me, but I don't want to look like this one, you know, with strips of-- If they'd beat me like that, I don't know. Maybe with razors, how they cut her. I don't know. Only not said. Tortures. They took me in. And there was a fat man sitting behind his desk, Chief Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a very good man. I tell you later about him. A very good man. But he was only sitting, writing down name and [INAUDIBLE]. Then two SS men-- these were the, was young men, tall young men-- they came in with huge big, big slices of bread and butter, so high butter on top. They thought maybe I want some food, and I tell them, I'll tell them my name if they offer me some. They ate it and came-- two men-- they came up to me and said again, what's your name? I said, Wanda Grabowienska. I could hardly bring me, you know, myself to talk to them. One hit me so hard on my face with his big hand, hit me when I said Wanda Grabowienska because he believed it's not my real name. Hit me so hard that he nearly got my eye out. It started to bleeding through the nose, and eye. My whole face was in blood. And that Chief Gestapo looked at it. He couldn't-- you know, he was a Chief Gestapo, but SS men, they had much more power than himself, those young men. You couldn't tell them stop. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't say, no. He didn't say anything. And they thought that I would start crying, because anyone, if you hit them so hard, so, but so hard that I felt the, a pain in the back of the head and blood running. And he thought-- they thought that now I will tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, what's your name? Wanda Grabowienska. So they couldn't believe that I'm Jewish. Jewish wouldn't be-- a Jewish one starts to cry, and tell them the truth, and begs them. Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where I took the strength. I don't know now. I just can't believe it was me then. I don't believe it. Then they let me, and I went back. I went back. They saw me all in blood. I don't know what I wore then. Because everything, you wore it, not washing, not doing anything. It-- it fell off nearly. With-- with-- with a-- a corner of my blouse, whatever I wore, I-- I just tried to clean up here and clean up the head. And that how it healed by itself, without anything went in. That-- once, middle of the night-- I told you about this-- they took me. And I thought they'd take me out to the backyard to kill me. No. They took me right down, right down, deep down in the building, opened the door, and threw me in. As I came in, I could hear someone-- I told you that, I think-- breathing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought that's, that's like this Rosicrucians in the Roman times, to the animals to eat them alive. Because I could hear breathing and I didn't-- it was dark there. I-- I could-- they closed quickly, because light was outside where they took me from, not to see anyone. They thought that will help, not only that whoever what animal is there will kill me. And I could hear hard breathing near me. And I didn't know, is it an animal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed it's an animal. It will tear me to pieces. But as I could hear it breathing here, I started-- it was a round cell-- I started to move around slowly, not to make a noise so it wouldn't hear me, and not to breathe. I stopped breathing, and that was a whole night. I don't believe it myself now. I can't believe it. I can't believe that's me. And I went around-- I tell you this story now, and it's true, and I don't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just moved slowly, slowly, not to make a noise, otherwise whoever was there, I couldn't believe it's a person. Because how could a person be here, and breathe so heavily, and not to say a word, without talking, without anything and breathing heavily? And I thought I can feel his breath on my arm here, and I moved around. And that went on a whole night. But that animal didn't catch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved slightly around till I don't know what time it was, early in the morning. The door opened quickly and they grabbed me, got me out. As they opened the door, I have seen a person, a woman, a woman more like an animal, naked, without anything. And I've seen the arm stretching out after me to catch me, and I've seen-- I looked and I've seen a-- blood all over her arms and her face. But she lived, and it was a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: They got me back to my cell, threw me in, and left. So the people inside told me later the-- the women, I said-- they said, were you there? Have you seen somebody else? I said, yes, when they took me out. I've scene a woman stretching her arms, with blood all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, she's one of the Russian officer's wife, one of them, a wife who, whom they took and they tortured her so badly that she wanted to commit suicide and cut her veins and arteries. And no doctor attended, but she was still alive. They didn't want her to die. They want her to suffer only, without food, without anything. And they locked her in there and that healed up, but that's what I have seen sticking out, like cut muscles sticking out. And that-- and they-- and she got mad there because of all the treatment, or anything-- everything what happened to her. She got mentally ill. She was mental. And they threw in to her, like myself, always another person. Some survived, others not. And she was very strong and very thin. What I have seen was not like a, like a-- a human being. She looked to me more like an animal. But she was kept there for-- for months and months. They didn't kill her. They want her to suffer or to-- to scare others. And then they got me out. And finally, when I came back, they all looked. They said, you, you survived her, others didn't. And then a day later-- get a headache-- a day later, they called me again. Whoever was called in to the Chief of Gestapo-- that was another room-- from our room, a door into there where they tortured people. They called me in and I thought another torture. But no. Somehow, when I came in-- a prisoner comes in, they took-- take away everything from me. Not only they, they take out from the pockets, but they took the whole coat. I remember I had a-- a green coat, and in the pocket, I kept the birth certificate which the Pole, Polish woman gave me, with my name as Wanda Grabowienska. There was it, in my pocket, and I forgot about it again. And they searched my coat, the pockets for anything else. Maybe I had some gold, maybe. Yes, and when I came in, they took off my ring, my wedding ring, and my watch. Whatever I had with me, they took away from me. And they did not-- the Chief of Gestapos that, that was there, but that, it's the SS men-- my coat. When I was tortured so many times, they just doubted if I'm Jewish or maybe not. They searched my coat, the coat which they took away from me. Everything, they took away from me. In the pocket, they found the birth certificate from the church and the minister who gave it, not to me, but they thought to me. It was given to the Polish woman whose home I stayed, who took my child from the hospital back, and she gave it to me, as I said before. So they found it. And as such, when, I lived as Wanda Grabowienska, the time that I lived during the war there. When they got it, there was the signature of the church and the minister. I don't, never knew his name. And to make sure that I'm the one, that I'm really Christian, they called him, not the minister. First, they-- before the minister, sorry. Before they called him, they called in two men, interpreters, a Pole and a Ukrainian. Because I said that I was-- my father was Polish, my mother, Ukrainian. That's why I spoke both languages. They called a Pole and a Ukrainian. You know, how far they went, one woman only. They called them in. They called me in to the Chief Gestapo's and they-- office of the Chief Gestapo. And that was on the same level-- from our room where we all were huddled together led a-- a door to the Chief Gestapo. They called-- I never knew what for. I thought they're going to torture me again. And I didn't feel anything. I didn't feel any pain, nothing. I didn't feel. Just my mind was blank. They called me in, and as I came in, they were standing, those two men.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of them started to talk to me in Polish. [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE] What school did-- did you finish? I said all the time that I'm a peasant girl, that I went only to-- to the fourth, as my parents, they're peasants. They couldn't afford sending me to school. And finally, I did only to the second class, primary, four years, only. So they said, what school did you go? I said, somewhere else. I said, in Brody, only for-- for-- in Polish. [NON-ENGLISH] Where did you learn such good Polish? I said, my father was Polish and we spoke Polish at home. [NON-ENGLISH] In Polish, everything. Chief Gestapo asked how is her grammar, Polish? She's-- her father was Polish. How is her-- He said, very good, very good. Finished. The Ukrainian said, your mother was Ukrainian. Yes. Why are you Polish? Because my mother-- my father wanted me to be Polish. And can you say-- How do you say patches, patches in Ukrainian? I said, yes. Can you say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I said it. I said it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you learn Ukrainian? My mother wanted me to be-- to, to know Ukraine. She spoke to me in Ukrainian. And in school, I said, in primary, we learn, here, Ukrainian, because, I said, a lot of Ukraines here. We say it's our Ukraine now, because it is now Ukraine. I said, yes. So they ask him again, how is her Ukraine? Very good. Finished, they left, and left me. That's to the Chief Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very clever man. He knew. I know that he knew. I felt it, that he knew. But he was a good man. He wouldn't do anything. Only the-- he couldn't tell them, stop doing it, because he was only Chief Gestapo. Then, suddenly, as I stood there before the-- I couldn't go alone myself, out. They have to take me out. They didn't take me. I-- they left me standing there. As I stood there, a priest came in. I never knew him. I haven't seen him, never. And I don't, didn't know, but the Polish woman, when she got me the certificate, told me where he was, in which church, and I know it. I know it exactly, and I know where it is, was a church.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, remember that was the church where I got you the certificate, and you are my sister. And she had to be about three years older than I am, so I was three years older and looked younger. As the priest-- I knew from childhood whenever-- in Poland, there were mostly those people-- you know, it was like in the, in the dark ages when a priest went by, it could be rain, it could be anything, they fell on their knees, on the street, grabbed his hand and kissed his hand. And maybe they do it now, still, they kiss the priest's hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I knew. I've seen it as a child. I've seen on the street. We live near a church, and I knew that, what they're doing. He came in, and as he stood near me, I fell on my knees, grabbed his hand and kissed his hand. As I kissed his hand, he put his hand to my-- never knew me. That was like a tragic comedy. He put his hand on my head, and said, child, don't be afraid. Got me up from my knees. Don't be afraid. I christened you. If he would say that-- no, they would kill him on the spot. So if he wanted to save me-- I don't know-- he saved himself, too. Got me up and said, child, don't be afraid. You'll go out of here. You don't have to be in prison. I christened you and you're a very good Catholic. You came-- I remember you exact-- very well. You came every Sunday to the mass. And talked to the Gestapo Chief and he said, yes. We have here, and you gave her. So he said, yes. Because one-- if one went-- wanted to work in German-- -- during the German occupation, one had to have a birth certificate to get work. Otherwise, they couldn't get anywhere. They want to be sure that he's not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, I gave it to her. He didn't give it to me. He gave it to the Polish woman. I gave it to her. She wanted to go to work. So I know to whom I gave it. I wouldn't give anyone else. Only to her, that I knew, and that I christened. That helped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: About a day later-- And he left. The Chief of Gestapo wrote down something, he left, and I was a good Christian. Next day, the men with a long paper, a roll of paper, and read something, Wanda Gru-- opened the door, Wanda Grabowienska, you're free. Go out. But I didn't go straight away. It's was stupid, what I did. There was a little picture of the Maria, the Holy Maria on the wall, which we're not-- we were not allowed to put it. But when no one saw it, the Germans, we put it on there. Because it was not allowed to believe, even, the Polish and Polish-- in a religion. And I fell on my knees and I wanted to thank her. Before I tell you about the songs that I learned there, they told me, Wanda, go quick, otherwise, if you don't go, they won't take you-- let you go anymore. So I got up, I went out, and went down the stairs, went out free. It was not the end of it. I was free. That priest helped me-- he helped himself and helped me. As I went out, I felt very sensitive. I felt that someone was behind me. I turned my head and there were two men from the police sent after me. They still didn't believe me. They still sent after me. Instead to go home to see my child, I didn't go, because I felt them, and I had one look and I-- I noticed a secret police, [NON-ENGLISH], and I didn't go. We passed the street, because that was the street. Had to go on foot, and I walked. And I walked down, and they were always behind me, and I knew why. I understood. I didn't go-- I passed the house where-- not there. I had to go into a street there, a smaller street. I didn't go to see my child, and I went straight to the church from [NON-ENGLISH] I remember, where the priest gave me that certificate and I went straight there. There, I sat down and I started to pray. These men waited for me. They didn't go out. They waited outside. And once I come out, we all go again. But I didn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The priest came in, and it wasn't that-- the same priest. It was another one. And I know there were a few there. Came in and understood, because everybody left and I was still sitting. He understood, and said to me, so have you got where to go? I said, no. He understood, said, where you going? Why are you sitting so long? No where to go. He took me quickly in and-- and sat me there in the cellar, again, but not for a long time, hid me there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how he found out through a window where that they have gone. He knew. He understood. He said, no one's there. You can go out. But I was afraid. I sat-- overnight I sat there. And dark, and I sat there. In the morning, he said, you can go out. And I said, I'm afraid to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, no one's there. You can go safely down. So I went out. And then, I still didn't go to my child. I sat for a while. I thought to go, maybe some-- I didn't know. I haven't seen these men. Maybe somebody else is behind me. And I was afraid to go home, because if I go home, they will find out there is a child, and whose child is it? They'll start asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go out. There was a bench. On the street, as you go there, there are a few trees and a bench there. And every Sunday-- it was a small place-- the soldiers-- that was in Poland-- the Polish soldiers used to dance with the peasant girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sat on the bench there, and I sat on the bench. You know who came back? These two men-- it was night time-- one is this, and one here, both sat there. They sat down near me, these two men that went after me, the police, and said, why are you sitting here? They didn't say, we remember you, that you went out of the prison. Just like that, why are you sitting here? Why don't you go home? I said, because I have no home. My home is in Brody, and I haven't got money to go for a train. Could you, could you help me out? Could you give me something, some money, then I will go home? They said, no, we have no money to give. We can't give you any money. Then I said, all right, so why-- why do you sit here? Leave me here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning-- it was late in the evening-- in the morning, I'll go. Maybe I'll find someone who will help me out. I want to go to Brody. Brody was a city further.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one started-- he thought that I would tell him to come nearer to me, you know, and advancing. I said, aren't you police? I know you are police people, aren't you? He said, yes. So I said, you know that's not a-- the German times, it was not allowed to rape anyone. I said, you know that. Why do you sit here with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I want you. I said, and I don't want you. I don't know where I took the courage then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. This is the end of Part 2. We'll continue the story in Part 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's way, when it was-- and-- and I went to my-- next day, I went there.
